1: mikets sheva-shanim ta'aseh shemitah (miqqēṣ šebaʿ-šānîm taʿăśeh šĕmiṭṭāh): 'At the end of every seven years you shall grant a release.' Shemittah from the root šāmaṭ (to let drop, to release, to let fall) — used elsewhere for letting a sword fall (2 Sam 6:6 variant) or releasing an animal. The sabbatical year (shnat hashemittah) is the seventh year in which debts between Israelites are released and the land is allowed to rest (Exod 23:10-11; Lev 25:1-7). The year of release images the Jubilee (Lev 25:10: deror, release/liberty) — which Jesus applies to his own ministry in Luke 4:18-19 (shevat shevuyim deror, proclaim liberty to captives). The whole sabbatical-Jubilee complex encodes the principle of periodic economic reset as a covenantal obligation.
11: ki lo-yechdal evyon mikerev ha'aretz al-ken anochi metzavecha lemor pato'ach tiftach et-yadcha le'achicha la'aniyecha ule'evyonecha be'artzecha (kî lōʾ-yeḥdal ʾebyôn miqqereb hāʾāreṣ ʿal-kēn ʾānōkî mĕṣawwĕkā lēmōr pātōaḥ tip̄taḥ ʾet-yādĕkā lĕʾāḥîkā lĕʿăniyyĕkā ûlĕʾebyônĕkā bĕʾarṣĕkā): 'For there will never cease to be poor in the land. Therefore I command you, You shall open wide your hand to your brother, to the needy and to the poor, in your land.' Jesus cites this verse twice: Matt 26:11 and Mark 14:7 (the poor you always have with you, but you will not always have me) — not as a fatalistic dismissal of poverty but as a citation of Deut 15:11 to make a Christological point about the unique and irreplaceable moment of his physical presence. The double infinitive absolute pātōaḥ tip̄taḥ (open wide, you shall open) is intensifying idiom — categorical generosity, not selective charity.
2: wezeh devar hashemittah shamot kol-baal masheh yado asher yashe bere'ehu: 'And this is how the release (šᵉmiṭṭāh) shall work: every creditor shall cancel (šāmôṭ) what he has lent to his neighbor.' šāmôṭ — the root šāmaṭ (to let drop, release, remit) is the same root used in Exod 23:11 for letting the land lie fallow in the seventh year. The debt-release mirrors the land-sabbath: as the land is released from cultivation every seventh year, so debts are released every seventh year. lo-yigos et-re'ehu ve'et-aḥiv (he must not press his neighbor or his brother) — the prohibition on pressing for payment uses the verb nāgas (to exact, press, drive), the same verb used of Pharaoh's slave-masters pressing Israel in Exodus (Exod 3:7; 5:6,10,13,14). The debt-release laws prevent the recreation of Egyptian bondage within Israel.
3: et-hanokri tiggos ve'asher yihyeh lekha et-aḥikha tashmet yadekha: 'You may collect from a foreigner, but whatever your brother owes you, you must release.' The distinction between nokrî (foreigner, non-resident alien) and ʾāḥ (brother, fellow Israelite) is an economic-covenantal distinction: the release operates within the covenant community, not universally. This has generated significant discussion in Jewish ethics about the scope of neighbor-obligation. The NT's expansion — 'Is he not also my neighbor?' (Luke 10:29-37) — challenges the limiting function of the ʾāḥ/nokrî distinction in the parable of the Good Samaritan.
4: efes ki lo-yihyeh bekha evyon ki-barekh yevarekhkha YHWH be'aretz asher YHWH Eloheicha noten lekha: 'There need be no poor among you, for the LORD will surely bless you in the land.' efes kî (except that, but) — the phrase introduces not a contradiction to v. 11 ('the poor will never cease,' which Jesus quotes in John 12:8) but a conditional vision: poverty is not structurally necessary in the covenant community if covenant obedience is maintained. The doubled blessing verb bārēk yᵉḇārᵉkekā (YHWH will surely bless you) — the Piel infinitive absolute + Piel finite — is the emphatic assurance of prosperity. The tension between v. 4 (need be no poor) and v. 11 (poor will always exist) is Deuteronomy's realistic covenant pedagogy: the ideal is stated, then the practical reality acknowledged.
5: raq im-shamo'a tishma bekol YHWH Eloheicha lishmor la'asot et-kol-hamitzvah hazot: 'if only you carefully obey the voice of the LORD your God, being diligent to keep all this commandment.' The doubled imperative šāmôaʿ tišmaʿ (hear-obey you shall hear-obey) — Hebrew's emphatic construction — qualifies the promise of v. 4: the blessing-prosperity is conditional on covenant faithfulness. šāmar laʿăśôt (guard to do) — the paired verbs of covenant faithfulness: guard (internalize, not forget) and do (implement). Deuteronomy consistently pairs these two verbs because knowledge without practice is covenant failure, and practice without understanding is mere external compliance.
6: ki-YHWH Eloheicha berakhekha kaasher dibber-lakh: 'For the LORD your God will bless you as he has promised.' kaʾăšer dibber-lāk (as he has spoken to you) — the blessing is not a new promise but the fulfillment of a prior word; the debt-release and generosity laws are embedded in a covenant where YHWH's promise precedes and underwrites the commands. vehaavet goyim rabbim ve'ata lo tilve (you will lend to many nations but not borrow) — the reversal of debtor-creditor status between Israel and the nations is the economic expression of covenant supremacy: the covenant people, when obedient, become the lenders rather than the borrowers. Paul applies this economic reversal spiritually in Phil 4:19: 'my God will supply every need of yours according to his riches in glory.'
7: ki-yihyeh vekha evyon me'aḥad aḥeicha be'aḥad she'arekha be'artzekha: 'If there is a poor person (ʾeḇyôn) among you, one of your brothers, in any of your towns.' ʾeḇyôn (needy, poor) — the specific term for one in want, in contrast to ānî (oppressed/humble-poor) and dal (weak/low). The combination of ʾeḇyôn and ʾāḥ (brother) personalizes the economic obligation: the poor person is not an abstract social problem but your brother, a member of the covenant family. lo-tĕ'ammēṣ ʾet-lᵉḇāḇᵉkā wᵉlōʾ tiqpōṣ ʾet-yāḏᵉkā mēʾāḥîkā hāʾeḇyôn (do not harden your heart or shut your hand against your poor brother) — the double prohibition uses body-part metaphors: the heart-hardening and hand-closing are the emotional and practical failures of covenant solidarity.
8: ki-phato'ah tiphtaḥ et-yadekha lo: 'Instead, you shall open your hand wide (pāṯôaḥ tipṯaḥ) to him.' The doubled infinitive absolute + finite verb construction (pāṯôaḥ tipṯaḥ — open wide you shall open wide) is the emphatic contrast to the hand-closing of v. 7. vehaavet taavit lo dei mahsoro asher yehsar lo (and lend him whatever he needs, enough for his lack) — mahsoro (his deficit) specifies that the loan amount is calibrated to need, not to lender-convenience. The open-hand imagery recurs in Ps 145:16 as a description of YHWH himself: 'you open your hand and satisfy the desire of every living thing' — Israel's open-handed generosity to the poor imitates YHWH's own provision-character.
9: hishamer lekha pen-yihyeh davar im-levavekha veliyyaal: 'Guard against an unworthy thought in your heart (dāḇār ʿim-lᵉḇāḇᵉkā beliyyāʿal).' beliyyāʿal — the compound term (literally ‘without profit/worth’) used for thoroughly wicked or worthless persons and things (cf. 'sons of Belial' in Judges, 1 Samuel, and later used as a name for Satan in 2 Cor 6:15). The thought prohibited here is calculating the approach of the shemittah year as a reason to withhold loans to the poor — using the release law as a cover for refusing generosity. Deuteronomy here commands the regulation of internal motivation, not merely external behavior: the problem is bᵉlibbekā (in your heart), not just in your actions.
10: naton tittennu lo velo-yera levavekha betitkha lo: 'Give to him generously (nāṯôn tiṯṯennû lô), and do not be reluctant in your heart when you give.' The doubled verb construction — nāṯôn tiṯṯennû (give you shall give) — insists on both external action and internal attitude: give, and do so without heart-resistance. kî biglal haddāḇār hazzeh yᵉḇārᵉkekā YHWH Eloheycha bᵉkol-maʿăśekā (for because of this the LORD your God will bless you in all your work) — the causal link between generosity and blessing is direct: the blessing flows from the giving, connecting the agrarian economy to covenant faithfulness.
12: ki-yimmakher lekha aḥikha haivri o haivriyvah va'avadkha shesh shanim: 'If your brother, a Hebrew man or a Hebrew woman, is sold to you and serves you six years.' The slave-manumission law (vv. 12-18) parallels Exod 21:2-6 with significant Deuteronomic expansions. The inclusion of haʿiḇriyyāh (the Hebrew woman) alongside the Hebrew man is Deuteronomy's egalitarian expansion of Exod 21:2, which applies only to male slaves. The six-year service + seventh-year release mirrors the agricultural sabbatical: as the land rests in the seventh year, so the Hebrew slave is released. The Exodus background is explicit in v. 15: 'you were a slave in Egypt.'
13: vekhiy-teshalleḥenu ḥofshi me'imakh lo teshalleḥenu reiqam: 'And when you set him free, you shall not send him away empty-handed (rêqām).' rêqām (empty, without resources) — the same word used of Rachel and Leah's complaint against Laban (Gen 31:42) and of Ruth's return to Bethlehem (Ruth 1:21; 3:17). Israel was sent out from Egypt not empty-handed but with the Egyptians' silver and gold (Exod 12:35-36); so the freed Hebrew slave must not be sent out empty-handed. The Exodus pattern governs the slave-release law: as YHWH enriched Israel at the Exodus, so Israelite masters must enrich their freed slaves.
14: ha'anet taaniq lo mitzanekha umigornekha umiqqevkha: 'You shall supply him generously from your flock, your threshing floor, and your winepress.' The three sources — flock, threshing floor (grain), winepress (wine) — represent the complete agricultural produce, the three pillars of ancient Israelite rural economy. ka'asher berakhkha YHWH Eloheicha titten lo (as the LORD your God has blessed you, give to him) — the measure of provision is calibrated to the master's own YHWH-given blessing: the freed slave receives in proportion to the master's prosperity. This divine-gift-flowing-through-human-agent pattern is foundational to the NT's theology of generous giving (2 Cor 9:7-8).
15: vezakharta ki eved hayita be'eretz mitzraim vayyiphdekhha YHWH Eloheicha: 'Remember that you were a slave in Egypt, and the LORD your God redeemed you.' The Exodus-memory motivation is Deuteronomy's single most repeated ethical driver: remember your former slavery and act accordingly toward those under you. al-ken anokhi metzavvekha et-haddavar hazeh hayom (that is why I command you this today) — the commandment is rooted in the gospel-narrative. The NT equivalent: 'be kind to one another, tenderhearted, forgiving one another, as God in Christ forgave you' (Eph 4:32) — the remembered experience of redemption is the basis for covenant generosity.
16: vekhiy-yomar elekha lo etze me'imakh ki ahavkha ve'et-beiteckha ki-tov lo imakh: 'But if the servant says, I do not want to leave you, because he loves you and your household and is well-off with you.' The voluntary-servitude provision addresses the servant who genuinely prefers continued service to freedom. ki ṭôb lô ʿimmāk (because it is good for him with you) — the master's household has become the servant's family; freedom would mean economic insecurity and social displacement. The option of permanent voluntary servitude (marked by ear-piercing) is not exploitation but an acknowledgment of the covenant household as a genuine community of belonging.
17: velaqaḥta et-hamartzeaa untatah be'ozno uvadelet: 'then you shall take an awl and pierce his ear against the door, and he shall be your servant permanently.' The ear-piercing ritual (marṣēaʿ, awl + door + ear) is a liminal act: the servant is symbolically bound to the household at its threshold (the door). wᵉʾap laʾamatkha taʿăśeh-kēn (do likewise for your female servant) — Deuteronomy's extension to the female servant ensures she too has the option of permanent covenant-household membership. The ritual of the pierced ear and door-post may echo the Passover blood on the doorpost: the household marked by YHWH's protection encompasses the servants within it.
18: lo yiqsheh beeinekha beshalleḥkha oto ḥofshi me'imakh: 'Do not find it difficult in your eyes when you set him free.' lo yiqšeh bᵉʿênekā (let it not be hard in your eyes) — Deuteronomy frequently addresses the inner resistance of the will: the law is not just about external compliance but about overcoming internal reluctance. ki mišneh sekhir sakhir avadkha shesh shanim (for he has served you six years at half the cost of a hired worker) — the economic argument: six years of Hebrew service at half the rate of a free hired worker means the master has already received good economic value; releasing the servant with a provision-gift is a fair return on an advantageous arrangement.
19: kol-habbekhor asher yivvaled bibekarkha uvatzankha hazkhar taqddish laYHWH Eloheicha: 'You shall set apart for YHWH your God every firstborn male born of your herd and your flock.' The firstborn-consecration law (vv. 19-23) is grounded in the Exodus: every firstborn belongs to YHWH because YHWH struck down Egypt's firstborn and spared Israel's (Exod 13:1-2,11-16). lo-ta'avod bevikhor shoreka velo tahoz bivikhor tzaonekha (do not work with the firstborn of your ox or shear the firstborn of your flock) — the firstborn animal is withdrawn from agricultural use: its consecrated status means it may not generate economic value for its owner; it is YHWH's working capital, not the farmer's.
20: lifnei YHWH Eloheicha tokhelennu shanah veshanah: 'Year by year you shall eat it before YHWH your God at the place YHWH will choose.' The firstborn-animal feast is annual and communal — eating before YHWH at the central sanctuary. ʾattāh ûḇêtekā (you and your household) — the feast includes the entire family. The annual eating of the consecrated firstborn before YHWH builds the household's covenant memory into the agricultural calendar: each year's new firstborn is both the sacrifice and the feast, consecrating the flock-cycle to YHWH's claim while providing communal celebration.
21: vekhiy-yihyeh vo mum pisseaḥ o iver kol mum ra lo tizbaḥennu laYHWH Eloheicha: 'But if it has any defect — if it is lame or blind or has any serious flaw — you do not sacrifice it to YHWH your God.' The defect-prohibition for firstborn sacrifice echoes the Levitical requirement for unblemished sacrifices (Lev 22:17-25). pissēaḥ ô ʿiwwēr kol mûm rāʿ (lame or blind or any serious defect) — the defect categories are physical disqualifications for altar-service. Malachi's polemic (Mal 1:8,13-14) condemns post-exilic Israel for offering defective animals — the standard Deuteronomy established was not maintained. The NT's 'without spot or blemish' language for Christ's sacrifice (1 Pet 1:19; Heb 9:14) invokes this sacrificial standard: the perfect sacrifice achieves what blemished animals could only represent.
23: akh et-damo lo tokhal al-haaretz tishpekhennu kamayim: 'Only do not eat its blood; pour it out on the ground like water.' The blood-prohibition — al-hāʾāreṣ tišpᵉkennû kammayim (pour it on the ground like water) — grounds the permission to eat unblemished firstborn animals with the fundamental condition: no blood. The blood is poured out because ki-haddām hu hannepeš (for the blood is the life, Deut 12:23; Lev 17:11,14). The blood-life equation is the theological rationale for all blood-prohibition: to eat blood is to consume life itself, which belongs to YHWH. The Jerusalem Council (Acts 15:20,29) retained the blood-prohibition as one of the four requirements for Gentile believers, reflecting its foundational status.
22: Eating the blemished firstborn locally: The defective firstborn that cannot be sacrificed at the central sanctuary is eaten within your gates — in the home town — and both the tam (ritually clean) and the tame' (ritually unclean) person may eat it together, like game (deer/gazelle). A disqualified sacrifice loses its sacred status and becomes ordinary food accessible to all, preventing waste while keeping the sanctuary standard intact. The parallel with Deut 12:15, 22 signals that blemished firstborns are assimilated to the class of non-sacrificial meat.
